title: The Common Law Tradition: Deciding Appeals
author: Karl N. Llewellyn
excerpt: Llewellyn's culminating defense of the common law's coherence and predictability, identifying fourteen steadying factors that constrain appellate judges and arguing for a return to the Grand Style of judging that balances legal craft with situation-sense.
keyIdeas: Fourteen steadying factors make appellate decision-making far more predictable than legal realism's critics feared
The Grand Style of judging balances precedent, principle, and policy overtly, while the Formal Style hides value choices behind mechanical rule-application
Precedent has leeways: sixty-four distinct techniques for handling prior cases, most legitimate
Situation-sense — a trained intuition for what fits a fact-pattern — guides judges toward just outcomes
The common law tradition is not a cage but a craft tradition that produces reckonable results without sacrificing flexibility
keyTakeaways: Appellate judging is constrained by institutional, doctrinal, professional, and collegial factors that skeptics underestimate
The Grand Style treats legal rules as living instruments whose rationale and purpose matter as much as their language
Advocates can improve their odds by understanding the steadying factors and period styles
The common law's coherence comes from craft tradition, not logical deduction from fixed rules
Llewellyn's analysis bridges the gap between legal realism's early iconoclasm and a constructive account of legal reasoning
whoShouldRead: Appellate judges and their law clerks seeking a framework for understanding judicial role
Practitioners who argue before appellate courts and want to understand how decisions are actually made
Legal scholars interested in legal realism, jurisprudence, and the nature of judicial discretion
Law students learning how precedent operates in a system of judge-made law
whoShouldSkip: Readers seeking a conventional casebook or procedural manual on appeals
Those looking for a short, straightforward introduction to American appellate practice
Readers put off by Llewellyn's idiosyncratic, dense prose style
